questionText: The times, $X$ hours, taken to fully charge a type of battery are modelled by $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. Records show that $12\%$ of batteries charge in under $10$ hours and $70\%$ charge in under $15$ hours. (a) Find $\mu$ and $\sigma$ to $3$ significant figures. (b) A charging station handles $200$ of these batteries in a day. Estimate how many charge in under $12$ hours.

steps.description: State the model and the two given facts
steps.explanation: Charging times are normal with unknown $\mu$ and $\sigma$. ‘$12\%$ under $10$ hours’ gives $P(X<10)=0.12$ and ‘$70\%$ under $15$ hours’ gives $P(X<15)=0.70$. Both are lower-tail proportions.
Standardising $10$ with $Z=\dfrac{X-\mu}{\sigma}$ keeps the lower-tail area at $0.12$.
With $12\%$ of the area below it, this point lies to the left of the mean, so the standardised value is negative.
Equating the standardised value of $10$ with $z_1$ gives the first equation.

steps.description: Standardise the second condition and read z
steps.explanation: The second statement is already lower-tail, so we standardise $15$ and read $z_2$. Since $70\%>50\%$ the value is positive.
Equating the standardised value of $15$ with $z_2$ gives the second simultaneous equation.
Subtracting the two ‘value $-\ \mu = z\sigma$’ forms removes $\mu$; the $5$-hour gap spans about $1.70$ standard deviations.
Dividing the time gap by the standardised gap gives a standard deviation of about $2.94$ hours.
From the first equation $\mu = 10 + 1.1750\,\sigma$. The mean charging time of about $13.5$ hours lies above $10$, consistent with only $12\%$ charging faster than $10$ hours.
To three significant figures the model is $X \sim N(13.5,\ 2.94^2)$ hours, answering part (a).

steps.description: Set up part (b): probability a battery charges in under 12 hours
steps.explanation: To estimate how many of the $200$ batteries charge in under $12$ hours we first find the single-battery probability, standardising $12$.
Since $12$ is a little below the mean of $13.5$, its standardised value is modestly negative.
The area to the left of a point just below the mean is a little under a half; here it is about $0.305$.
For $200$ batteries the expected number charging in under $12$ hours is the sample size times the single-battery probability.
So about $61$ of the $200$ batteries are expected to charge in under $12$ hours.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: (a) $\mu \approx 13.5$ hours, $\sigma \approx 2.94$ hours. (b) about $61$ of the $200$ batteries.
